name: Chris Mann
education: Law degree (school not specified in public records)
family: Wife Ashley is a surgeon practicing in Olathe
whoTheyAre:
Chris Mann is an attorney from Lawrence, Kansas, who is running for attorney general as a Democrat. He started his career as a police officer at age 21, while still in college. In 2002, when he was 25, a drunk driver hit him during a traffic stop, and the injuries forced him to retire from law enforcement. After that, he went to law school and became a prosecutor in Wyandotte County. He later worked at the Kansas Securities Commission and opened his own private law firm in Lawrence in 2016. He also served as the national chairman of Mothers Against Drunk Driving (MADD), where he pushed for Kansas to adopt an ignition lock law for repeat DUI offenders.

Mann ran for attorney general in 2022 and came closer than almost anyone expected. He lost to Kris Kobach by only about 15,000 votes out of more than one million cast, a margin of just 1.6 percent. That was the closest statewide race in Kansas that year. His wife, Ashley, is a surgeon who practices in Olathe. His campaign treasurer is Jill Docking, who was a Democratic candidate for lieutenant governor in 2014.

Mann announced his second run for attorney general on July 30, 2025. His pitch is straightforward: the attorney general's office should focus on public safety, the Constitution, and everyday courtroom work instead of chasing national headlines. He has never held elected office.
theirRecord:
Because Mann has never held elected office, his public record is professional rather than legislative. His career in law enforcement and prosecution is the backbone of his candidacy. As a police officer, he worked the streets until a drunk driver ended that chapter of his life. As a prosecutor in Wyandotte County, he handled criminal cases. His work with MADD gave him a national platform on DUI prevention and victims' rights, and he advocated for stronger drunk driving laws in Kansas.

Mann's 2022 race against Kobach is the most significant entry on his political record. He ran as a first-time candidate with low name recognition in a state that leans heavily Republican, and he came within 1.6 points of winning. That close result, combined with Kobach's polarizing record as attorney general, is the main reason Mann is running again. His campaign frames the race as a choice between a practical, public-safety-focused attorney general and one who prioritizes national political fights over the basics of law enforcement.

Critics would note that Mann has no legislative voting record to examine and no track record of managing a large government office. His case rests entirely on his professional background and the argument that the current attorney general has been more interested in partisan lawsuits than in protecting Kansas families.
whatYouShouldKnow: Mann lost to Kobach by only 1.6 percent in 2022, making it the closest statewide race in Kansas that cycle. This is a direct rematch.
He became a police officer at 21 and was forced to retire at 25 after being hit by a drunk driver during a traffic stop.
He served as national chairman of Mothers Against Drunk Driving (MADD) and has been on the national board since 2014.
His 2026 campaign has raised $536,639.57 with $257,257.08 cash on hand, showing serious statewide fundraising ability.
He has never held elected office. His experience is entirely professional: police officer, prosecutor, and private attorney.

Governor Laura Kelly's staff reportedly pressured other Democratic candidates to clear the field for Mann, according to state senator Cindy Holscher, who said she was told 'do not run because Chris Mann's the guy.'
His campaign message centers on refocusing the attorney general's office on public safety and courtroom work rather than national political battles.
His wife Ashley is a surgeon, and his campaign treasurer is Jill Docking, a former Democratic lieutenant governor candidate.
whereTheyWorship: No public church affiliation could be verified for Chris Mann in the sources reviewed. His campaign materials reference faith in general terms, saying he 'answered the call of his faith,' but no specific congregation, denomination, or church website was identified. If he worships privately, that information was not part of his public-facing campaign or media profile as of the research date.

quotes.text: Chris Mann has dedicated his life to serving and protecting Kansas communities - first as a police officer and then as a prosecutor.
He will defend the Constitution, protect the rule of law, and ensure Kansas families are safe.
Defend the rule of law, protect our rights, protect our families and make Kansas a safer place to live.

campaignFinance.narrative: Mann's campaign finance report shows a serious statewide operation for a challenger. He raised $536,639.57 and spent $280,041.49, leaving $257,257.08 cash on hand. The filing also showed $7,655.77 in in-kind support and no personal loans. His donor base is broad, with many small-to-mid-sized contributions from individual Kansas donors rather than a single dominant funding source. The mix of lawyers, suburban professionals, and grassroots supporters fits the campaign's message that Mann is building a people-powered alternative.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: No undisclosed or dark money sources were identified in the filing reviewed.
